summary:
Delft Pottery (Delftware, c. 1600–1800 AD) was the distinctive blue-and-white tin-glazed earthenware produced in the city of Delft in the Dutch Republic that became one of the [[event:2326|Dutch Golden Age's]] most iconic artistic and commercial exports — initially a European imitation of imported Chinese porcelain, it evolved into a distinctive Dutch art form that influenced ceramic traditions across Europe. As the [[event:2357|Dutch East India Company's]] imports of Chinese Ming porcelain flooded the Dutch market in the early 17th century, Dutch pottery workshops began imitating the blue-on-white aesthetic with local tin-glazed earthenware. When the decline of Ming china production temporarily interrupted Chinese imports in the 1620s-1640s, Delft manufacturers filled the gap with their own product.

Delft workshops reached peak production and artistic achievement in the mid-17th century, employing hundreds of workers and producing an enormous variety of objects: tiles depicting scenes of Dutch daily life, large decorative vases in Chinese style, tulip vases (reflecting the [[event:2510|tulip mania]] era), and iconic blue-and-white plates and jugs with Dutch scenes. The De Porceleyne Fles (Royal Delft), founded in 1653, is the only surviving original Delft factory still producing traditional ware. Delftware's technical imitation of porcelain spurred European search for the secret of true porcelain manufacture, eventually achieved by Johann Friedrich Böttger at Meissen in 1708, which created the European porcelain industry. Dutch Delftware tiles, widely exported, can still be found in historic buildings from Portugal to Sweden, a testament to Dutch commercial reach during the Golden Age.
